events.title: Partition of British India
events.detail: The British Indian Empire was partitioned into two independent dominions — India and Pakistan — as the Indian Independence Act came into force on August 14–15, 1947. The Radcliffe Line divided Punjab and Bengal, triggering the largest mass migration in human history with an estimated 10–20 million displaced. Communal violence claimed between 200,000 and 2 million lives.

events.title: Pakistan Established as Independent Dominion
events.detail: Pakistan became independent on August 14, 1947, with Muhammad Ali Jinnah as Governor-General. The new state comprised West Pakistan (present-day Pakistan) and East Pakistan (present-day Bangladesh), separated by 1,600 km of Indian territory. Jinnah's vision of a Muslim homeland was realized but accompanied by catastrophic communal violence.

events.title: Maharaja Hari Singh Signs Instrument of Accession to India
events.detail: Under pressure from Pakistani-backed tribal militias advancing on Srinagar, Maharaja Hari Singh of Jammu and Kashmir signed the Instrument of Accession to India on October 26, 1947. India accepted the accession on the condition of holding a plebiscite once law and order was restored — a plebiscite Pakistan argues was never held, and India contends became moot after Pakistan's aggression.

events.title: Pakistani Tribal Forces Invade Kashmir (First Kashmir War)
events.detail: Thousands of Pakistani Pashtun tribal fighters (lashkars), supported by elements of the Pakistani military, invaded Kashmir from the northwest on October 22, 1947. They captured Muzaffarabad and advanced toward Srinagar. The invasion triggered the Maharaja's accession to India and the first of four major India-Pakistan wars.

events.title: Indian Troops Airlifted to Srinagar
events.detail: Within hours of the Maharaja signing the accession, Indian Army troops of the 1st Sikh Regiment were airlifted into Srinagar airport on October 27, 1947 — the first time an airlift was used in combat operations in the subcontinent. The troops halted the tribal advance just outside Srinagar and began pushing them back.

events.title: UN Security Council Calls for Ceasefire in Kashmir
events.detail: The UN Security Council passed Resolution 39 on January 17, 1948, calling for a ceasefire in Kashmir and establishing the UN Commission for India and Pakistan (UNCIP). The resolution called for the withdrawal of Pakistani forces and tribesmen, and eventually a free and impartial plebiscite under UN auspices. Subsequent resolutions laid out conditions that were never fulfilled.

events.title: Ceasefire Line Established in Kashmir
events.detail: A UN-brokered ceasefire came into effect on January 1, 1949, ending the First Kashmir War. The ceasefire line divided Kashmir, with India holding approximately two-thirds (Jammu, Kashmir Valley, and Ladakh) and Pakistan holding one-third (Azad Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan). This line later became the Line of Control (LOC) after the 1972 Simla Agreement.

events.title: Karachi Agreement Formalizes Ceasefire Line
events.detail: The Karachi Agreement of July 27, 1949 formally defined the ceasefire line in Kashmir between Indian and Pakistani forces, witnessed by UNCIP military advisers. The agreement designated a demilitarized zone in some areas but the broader issue of a plebiscite remained unresolved. This document remained the foundational border instrument until the 1972 Simla Agreement.

events.title: Pakistan Joins SEATO and Aligns with Western Powers
events.detail: Pakistan joined the Southeast Asia Treaty Organization (SEATO) in 1954 and the Central Treaty Organization (CENTO/Baghdad Pact) in 1955, aligning itself firmly with the United States during the Cold War. This military partnership provided Pakistan with substantial US military aid, which India viewed as a threat to regional balance and its own security.

events.title: Indus Waters Treaty Signed Between India and Pakistan
events.detail: The Indus Waters Treaty was signed by Indian PM Jawaharlal Nehru and Pakistani President Ayub Khan in Karachi on September 19, 1960, brokered by the World Bank. The treaty allocated three eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and three western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan. It has survived three wars and remains one of the most successful international water-sharing agreements.

events.title: Sino-Indian War Reshapes Regional Balance
events.detail: China's military victory over India in the October–November 1962 border war humiliated New Delhi and fundamentally altered South Asian geopolitics. Pakistan saw strategic opportunity in India's weakness and deepened ties with China. The China-Pakistan alignment that emerged from this period shaped the India-Pakistan-China triangular rivalry that persists to the present day.

events.title: Rann of Kutch Skirmish
events.detail: Pakistani forces clashed with Indian border police and army units in the disputed Rann of Kutch region in April 1965. Pakistan made territorial gains in this swampy borderland before a UK-brokered ceasefire in June 1965. Pakistan interpreted India's restrained response as a sign of weakness and proceeded to plan Operation Gibraltar in Kashmir.

events.title: Pakistan Launches Operation Gibraltar (Infiltration into Kashmir)
events.detail: Pakistan launched Operation Gibraltar in August 1965, infiltrating thousands of fighters into Indian-administered Kashmir to foment an uprising against Indian rule. The plan was conceived by Pakistani General Yahya Khan and the operation was intended to be covert. However, local Kashmiris reported the infiltrators to Indian authorities, causing the operation to fail and triggering full-scale war.

events.title: Second Indo-Pakistani War Begins (Operation Grand Slam / Op Riddle)
events.detail: Pakistan launched Operation Grand Slam on September 1, 1965, attacking the Chamb-Akhnoor sector to cut Indian supply lines to Kashmir. India retaliated on September 6 by crossing the international border toward Lahore. The 17-day war saw the largest tank battles since World War II in the Sialkot and Lahore sectors. Both sides made modest territorial gains before a UN-mandated ceasefire.

events.title: Tashkent Declaration Ends 1965 War
events.detail: Indian PM Lal Bahadur Shastri and Pakistani President Ayub Khan signed the Tashkent Declaration on January 10, 1966, mediated by Soviet Premier Kosygin. Both sides agreed to withdraw to pre-war positions, release POWs, and restore diplomatic relations. Shastri died of a heart attack hours after signing. The declaration was criticized in India for not delivering a decisive outcome.

events.title: Pakistan Army Launches Operation Searchlight in East Pakistan
events.detail: The Pakistani Army launched Operation Searchlight on March 25, 1971 — a brutal crackdown on Bengali nationalist movement in East Pakistan. Thousands of civilians, intellectuals, Hindu minorities, and Awami League members were killed. The operation triggered a massive refugee crisis, with an estimated 10 million Bengalis fleeing to India, placing immense pressure on New Delhi to intervene.

events.title: Third India-Pakistan War Begins
events.detail: Pakistan launched pre-emptive airstrikes against Indian airbases on December 3, 1971, hoping to neutralize IAF power. India responded with a two-front offensive — in East Pakistan and along the western border. The Indian Army, together with the Mukti Bahini (Bangladesh liberation forces), rapidly advanced on Dhaka. The war was the most decisive of the three conflicts.

events.title: Pakistani Forces Surrender at Dhaka — Bangladesh Created
events.detail: Pakistan's Lt. Gen. A.A.K. Niazi signed the instrument of surrender on December 16, 1971, surrendering to Indian Lt. Gen. Jagjit Singh Aurora and Indian/Bangladeshi joint forces. Approximately 93,000 Pakistani soldiers became prisoners of war — the largest military surrender since World War II. East Pakistan became the independent nation of Bangladesh.

events.title: Simla Agreement — LOC Established
events.detail: Indian PM Indira Gandhi and Pakistani PM Zulfikar Ali Bhutto signed the Simla Agreement on July 2, 1972. The agreement transformed the 1949 ceasefire line into the Line of Control (LOC), committed both sides to resolve disputes bilaterally, and established diplomatic normalization. India returned 93,000 Pakistani POWs. The Simla Agreement remains the foundational bilateral framework.

events.title: India Conducts First Nuclear Test — 'Smiling Buddha'
events.detail: India detonated its first nuclear device, codenamed 'Smiling Buddha,' at Pokhran, Rajasthan on May 18, 1974. The official designation was a 'peaceful nuclear explosion.' The test alarmed Pakistan and accelerated Islamabad's own nuclear weapons program. Pakistani PM Zulfikar Ali Bhutto vowed to build a nuclear bomb even if Pakistanis had to 'eat grass.'

events.title: Pakistan Establishes Kahuta Enrichment Facility — Nuclear Program Accelerates
events.detail: Pakistan's A.Q. Khan Research Laboratories were established at Kahuta in 1976, based on uranium enrichment technology Khan had stolen from URENCO in the Netherlands. The facility became the heart of Pakistan's nuclear weapons program. US intelligence began tracking the program in the late 1970s, leading to sanctions that were periodically waived due to Cold War strategic interests.

events.title: India Seizes Siachen Glacier — Operation Meghdoot
events.detail: India launched Operation Meghdoot on April 13, 1984, airlifting troops to occupy the Siachen Glacier in Ladakh before Pakistan could do the same. At altitudes of 5,000–6,200 meters, Siachen became the world's highest battlefield. Both India and Pakistan have maintained permanent military presence there ever since. Thousands of soldiers have died from avalanches and extreme cold rather than combat.

events.title: Operation Brasstacks — Largest Indian Military Exercise Creates Crisis
events.detail: India's Operation Brasstacks (November 1986 – January 1987) was the largest military exercise in South Asian history, involving over 400,000 troops near the Pakistani border. Pakistan mobilized counter-forces in response. The crisis escalated to near-war before being defused through back-channel diplomacy. The incident prompted both sides to develop nuclear coercion frameworks.

events.title: Kashmir Insurgency Begins — JKLF and Militant Uprising
events.detail: Armed insurgency erupted in Indian-administered Kashmir in December 1989, initially driven by the Jammu Kashmir Liberation Front (JKLF) seeking independence. Pakistan began providing training, arms, and support to various militant groups. The Indian security forces conducted counter-insurgency operations that led to widespread human rights abuses. The conflict killed tens of thousands over the following decades.

events.title: Mass Exodus of Kashmiri Pandits
events.detail: Hundreds of thousands of Kashmiri Pandits (Hindu Kashmiris) fled the Kashmir Valley in January–February 1990 amid targeted killings, threats, and intimidation from Islamist militants. Estimates range from 100,000 to 500,000 displaced. The exodus remains one of the most contentious events in the Kashmir conflict, cited by India as evidence of ethnic cleansing and contested by Pakistani and some Kashmiri nationalist accounts.

events.title: India Conducts Pokhran-II Nuclear Tests — Operation Shakti
events.detail: India conducted five nuclear tests at Pokhran on May 11–13, 1998 (Operation Shakti), declaring itself a nuclear weapons state. PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee announced the tests publicly. The tests triggered international sanctions from the US, Japan, and others. Pakistan's nuclear tests followed within two weeks, completing South Asia's overt nuclear standoff.

events.title: Pakistan Conducts Chagai Nuclear Tests
events.detail: Pakistan detonated five nuclear devices at the Chagai Hills, Balochistan on May 28, 1998, followed by a sixth test on May 30. PM Nawaz Sharif declared Pakistan a nuclear state. The tests were met with jubilation in Pakistan and consternation internationally. South Asia formally became a nuclear-armed region, fundamentally altering the strategic calculus of any future India-Pakistan conflict.

events.title: Lahore Declaration — India-Pakistan Peace Initiative
events.detail: Indian PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee traveled to Lahore by bus on February 20–21, 1999, meeting Pakistani PM Nawaz Sharif and signing the Lahore Declaration. The declaration committed both nations to peaceful resolution of disputes, including Kashmir, through bilateral dialogue. The historic gesture raised hopes for normalization, but the Kargil War erupted just three months later.

events.title: Pakistani Forces Infiltrate Kargil Heights
events.detail: During the winter of 1998–99, Pakistani Army soldiers and paramilitary fighters from the Northern Light Infantry crossed the Line of Control and occupied strategic peaks in the Kargil district of Ladakh, exploiting the annual withdrawal of Indian Army posts during winter. The infiltrators were officially described by Pakistan as Kashmiri militants, but were subsequently confirmed to be regular Pakistani military personnel.

events.title: Indian Army Discovers Kargil Intrusion
events.detail: Indian shepherds and army patrols discovered the Pakistani occupation of Kargil peaks on May 3, 1999. Initial Indian reconnaissance patrols were ambushed and suffered casualties. As the scale of intrusion became clear — over 130 peaks occupied across an 80 km front — India launched Operation Vijay to evict the intruders.

events.title: India Launches Operation Vijay — IAF Enters Kargil
events.detail: India launched Operation Vijay to retake the occupied peaks, and on May 26, the Indian Air Force entered the conflict for the first time, flying strikes against Pakistani positions. The IAF operated under strict orders to remain on the Indian side of the LOC. India lost several aircraft to Pakistani Stinger missiles and MANPADS, including MiG-21, MiG-27, and Mi-8 helicopter.

events.title: India Recaptures Tiger Hill — Key Kargil Peak
events.detail: Indian Army's 18 Grenadiers regiment recaptured Tiger Hill (Point 5062) near Drass on July 4, 1999, in a grueling high-altitude battle. Tiger Hill was one of the most strategically significant peaks as it overlooked the Srinagar-Leh National Highway. Its recapture marked the turning point of the Kargil War and was celebrated across India.

events.title: Nawaz Sharif Meets Clinton in Washington — Pakistan Agrees to Withdraw
events.detail: Pakistani PM Nawaz Sharif flew to Washington on July 4, 1999 (American Independence Day) and met US President Clinton. Under intense American pressure, Sharif agreed to withdraw Pakistani forces to the LOC. The withdrawal was seen as a humiliating climbdown in Pakistan and contributed to the October 1999 military coup. India insisted on complete withdrawal before any talks.

events.title: Kargil War Ends — LOC Fully Restored (Vijay Diwas)
events.detail: By July 26, 1999, India had evicted Pakistani forces from all occupied Indian territory, restoring the Line of Control. India declared July 26 as 'Vijay Diwas' (Victory Day). The Kargil conflict cost India approximately 527 soldiers killed and Pakistan an estimated 350–1,000 killed (Pakistan disputes exact figures). It was the first conventional war between two nuclear-armed states.

events.title: General Musharraf Overthrows Nawaz Sharif in Military Coup
events.detail: Pakistani Army Chief Gen. Pervez Musharraf overthrew PM Nawaz Sharif in a bloodless coup on October 12, 1999, while Sharif was attempting to dismiss Musharraf and divert his returning aircraft. Musharraf, one of the architects of the Kargil operation, became Chief Executive and later President. The coup derailed the nascent India-Pakistan peace process and set back democratic governance in Pakistan.

events.title: Agra Summit — Vajpayee-Musharraf Talks Fail
events.detail: Indian PM Vajpayee and Pakistani President Musharraf met at the Agra Summit in July 2001 in an attempt to break the diplomatic stalemate. The talks collapsed over the core issue of Kashmir — Pakistan demanding it be central to any joint declaration, India insisting on addressing cross-border terrorism first. The failure dashed hopes for a breakthrough in bilateral relations.

events.title: Attack on Indian Parliament — Near-War Crisis
events.detail: Five gunmen from Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) and Jaish-e-Mohammed (JeM) attacked the Indian Parliament building in New Delhi on December 13, 2001. All five attackers were killed but 9 security personnel died. India blamed Pakistan-based militant groups and launched Operation Parakram — the largest Indian military mobilization since 1971. The standoff lasted over a year with both sides mobilizing nearly 1 million troops.

events.title: Operation Parakram — India-Pakistan Military Standoff
events.detail: India launched Operation Parakram in January 2002, mobilizing over 500,000 troops along the Pakistan border and LOC in response to the Parliament attack. Pakistan responded with its own mobilization. The standoff lasted nearly a year and involved multiple nuclear signaling episodes. An estimated 800 Indian soldiers died from accidents, landmines, and disease during the standoff — without a shot fired.

events.title: Composite Dialogue Process Resumes
events.detail: India and Pakistan agreed to resume the Composite Dialogue process on January 6, 2004, at the SAARC Summit in Islamabad. Musharraf committed to not allowing Pakistani territory to be used for terrorism against India. The dialogue covered eight baskets: peace and security, Siachen, Wullar Barrage, Sir Creek, terrorism, drug trafficking, economic cooperation, and people-to-people contacts.

events.title: Mumbai Train Bombings — 209 Killed
events.detail: Seven coordinated bomb blasts struck Mumbai's commuter rail system on July 11, 2006 during peak hours, killing 209 people and wounding over 700. Indian investigators attributed the attacks to Pakistani militant group Lashkar-e-Taiba with assistance from the Students Islamic Movement of India (SIMI). The attacks suspended the Composite Dialogue process.

events.title: Mumbai Attacks (26/11) — 166 Killed in 60-Hour Siege
events.detail: Ten Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) gunmen launched coordinated attacks across Mumbai on November 26, 2008, targeting the Taj Hotel, Oberoi Hotel, Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus, Nariman House, and other sites. The 60-hour siege killed 166 people (including 6 Americans) and wounded 300+. Nine attackers were killed; sole survivor Ajmal Kasab was captured, tried, and executed in 2012. Pakistan-based LeT operational commander Zaki-ur-Rehman Lakhvi was implicated.

events.title: Pakistan Arrests LeT Commanders After Mumbai Pressure
events.detail: Under intense international pressure following 26/11, Pakistan arrested several Lashkar-e-Taiba commanders including alleged operational planner Zaki-ur-Rehman Lakhvi. However, subsequent trials collapsed repeatedly due to lack of evidence presented by prosecutors. Lakhvi was released on bail multiple times and remained a point of contention in India-Pakistan relations for over a decade.

events.title: LOC Ceasefire Violations Spike — Beheading Incident
events.detail: LOC ceasefire violations increased dramatically in 2013, with the Indian Army reporting over 200 violations during the year. In January 2013, Pakistani soldiers allegedly beheaded an Indian soldier, Lance Naik Hemraj, triggering public outrage in India. Pakistan denied responsibility. The incidents strained the bilateral ceasefire agreement that had been in place since 2003.

events.title: Modi Invites Pakistani PM to His Inauguration
events.detail: Indian PM Narendra Modi made an unprecedented diplomatic gesture by inviting Pakistani PM Nawaz Sharif and all SAARC leaders to his inauguration on May 26, 2014. Sharif accepted and attended. The gesture signaled Modi's initial intent to reset ties through a 'neighbors first' policy, though the relationship subsequently deteriorated amid continued terrorist incidents.

events.title: Pathankot Air Force Base Attack
events.detail: Six gunmen from Jaish-e-Mohammed (JeM) attacked the Pathankot Air Force Station in Punjab on January 2, 2016. The 72-hour siege killed 7 Indian security personnel and all 6 attackers. The attack came weeks after Modi's surprise visit to PM Nawaz Sharif in Lahore. India provided Pakistan with evidence implicating JeM's Masood Azhar; Pakistan's investigation made little progress.

events.title: Uri Army Camp Attack — 19 Indian Soldiers Killed
events.detail: Four militants attacked the Indian Army Brigade HQ at Uri in Baramulla district on September 18, 2016 at 5:30 AM, throwing grenades into sleeping barracks. The attack killed 19 Indian soldiers — the deadliest militant attack on the Indian Army in two decades. All four militants were killed. India attributed the attack to Jaish-e-Mohammed, Pakistan denied involvement.

events.title: India Conducts Surgical Strikes Across LOC
events.detail: Indian Army Special Forces conducted surgical strikes against militant launch pads across the LOC in Pakistan-administered Kashmir on the night of September 28–29, 2016. India claimed to have destroyed seven terrorist camps and inflicted significant casualties. Pakistan denied any Indian troops crossed the LOC, calling the claim a fabrication. The strikes marked a significant escalation in India's declared willingness to strike across the LOC.

events.title: Pulwama Suicide Bombing — 40 CRPF Personnel Killed
events.detail: A Jaish-e-Mohammed (JeM) suicide bomber named Adil Ahmad Dar detonated an explosive-laden vehicle next to a CRPF convoy on the Jammu-Srinagar National Highway at Pulwama on February 14, 2019. The blast killed 40 CRPF personnel — the deadliest militant attack in Kashmir in 30 years. JeM claimed responsibility; India blamed Pakistan for harboring JeM.

events.title: India Strikes Balakot — First IAF Attack in Pakistan Since 1971
events.detail: Indian Air Force Mirage 2000 jets struck a Jaish-e-Mohammed training camp near Balakot in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, on February 26, 2019 — the first Indian airstrike inside Pakistan proper since the 1971 war. India claimed to have killed 300+ militants; Pakistan admitted no such casualties and disputed the outcome. International observers noted uncertainty over actual damage inflicted.

events.title: Pakistan Shoots Down IAF MiG-21 — Wing Commander Abhinandan Captured
events.detail: The day after the Balakot strike, Pakistan Air Force jets entered Indian airspace; India scrambled fighters including a MiG-21 Bison piloted by Wing Commander Abhinandan Varthaman. PAF claims he was shot down in aerial combat (Pakistan also lost an F-16, disputed by Islamabad). Abhinandan ejected over Pakistani territory and was captured by local villagers. The incident brought both nuclear powers to the brink of broader war.

events.title: Pakistan Releases Wing Commander Abhinandan as 'Peace Gesture'
events.detail: Pakistani PM Imran Khan announced in parliament on February 28, 2019 that Wing Commander Abhinandan would be released as a 'peace gesture.' He crossed back to India at the Wagah border on March 1, 2019 to a hero's welcome. The crisis de-escalated, but both sides remained at high alert. The episode highlighted both the escalation risks and the importance of back-channel communication.

events.title: India Revokes Article 370 — Jammu & Kashmir Reorganized
events.detail: India revoked Article 370 of the Constitution on August 5, 2019, stripping Jammu & Kashmir of its special status. The state was bifurcated into two Union Territories: J&K and Ladakh. The communication blackout and security lockdown that followed lasted months. Pakistan strongly condemned the move, downgraded diplomatic relations, suspended trade, and raised the issue at the UN General Assembly.

events.title: Galwan Valley India-China Clash — India Faces Two-Front Challenge
events.detail: The India-China clash in Galwan Valley (June 15–16, 2020), killing 20 Indian and 4+ Chinese soldiers, created a two-front strategic challenge for India. India now faces simultaneous pressure from both China in Ladakh and Pakistan along the LOC/border. This shaped India's military procurement priorities, accelerated modernization, and led India to further consolidate ties with the US.

events.title: India-Pakistan Agree to Reinstate 2003 LOC Ceasefire
events.detail: India and Pakistan announced through a joint statement by their Director Generals of Military Operations (DGMOs) on February 25, 2021 that they had agreed to strictly observe the 2003 ceasefire agreement along the LOC and all other sectors. The agreement, brokered partly through UAE back-channel mediation, dramatically reduced LOC violations which had numbered over 5,000 in 2020.

events.title: Pakistan's Economic Crisis — IMF Bailout Constrains Strategic Options
events.detail: Pakistan faced a severe economic crisis in 2022–2023, with inflation exceeding 30%, foreign exchange reserves near depleted, and the country near default. An IMF bailout of $3 billion was secured in July 2023. The economic distress, combined with political turmoil following Imran Khan's ouster in April 2022, significantly constrained Pakistan's strategic options vis-à-vis India.

events.title: Imran Khan Arrested, PTI Crackdown Deepens Political Instability
events.detail: Former Pakistani PM Imran Khan was arrested in August 2023 and later sentenced to prison on multiple charges in what he and his supporters called politically motivated prosecutions. The crackdown on his Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI) party, deep political polarization, and Army intervention in politics created unprecedented instability. The internal crisis absorbed Pakistani political focus away from India policy.

events.title: Pahalgam Tourist Attack — 26 Killed in Kashmir
events.detail: Militants attacked tourists at Baisaran meadow near Pahalgam in the Kashmir Valley on April 22, 2025, killing 26 people including tourists. The Resistance Front, linked to Lashkar-e-Taiba, initially claimed responsibility but later retracted. India blamed Pakistan-backed militants and suspended the Indus Waters Treaty, recalled its High Commissioner, and imposed further trade restrictions. Pakistan denied involvement.

events.title: India Suspends Indus Waters Treaty Following Pahalgam Attack
events.detail: India announced the suspension of the 1960 Indus Waters Treaty on April 24, 2025 in response to the Pahalgam attack, the most dramatic step in 65 years of bilateral water management. India also closed the Integrated Check Post at Attari-Wagah, revoked Pakistani visas, and downgraded diplomatic representation. Pakistan declared the suspension of the treaty illegal under international law.

events.title: India Launches Operation Sindoor — Strikes JeM/LeT Camps in Pakistan
events.detail: India launched Operation Sindoor on May 7, 2025, striking nine militant infrastructure targets in Pakistan and Pakistan-administered Kashmir in response to the Pahalgam attack. Indian precision strikes hit JeM bases in Bahawalpur and LeT facilities in Muridke. Pakistan responded with artillery and drone strikes across the LOC, killing dozens on both sides. A ceasefire brokered by the US and Saudi Arabia came into effect on May 10.

events.title: US-Brokered Ceasefire Halts India-Pakistan Exchange
events.detail: A US and Saudi Arabia-mediated ceasefire between India and Pakistan came into effect on May 10, 2025, following the most serious military exchange between the two nuclear powers since Kargil. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Saudi Arabia's diplomatic back-channel reportedly played key roles. Both sides claimed success; the underlying triggers — Kashmir, cross-border militancy — remained unresolved.
